models.description: The Alpine A110, produced from 1961 to 1977, is the lightweight rear-engined sports car that became a rally legend. Its fibreglass body over a tubular backbone chassis, combined with Renault-derived engines, delivered exceptional agility. Victory at the 1973 Monte Carlo Rally cemented its place in motorsport history.

The Alpine A110, relaunched in 2017, revives the lightweight mid-engined sports car philosophy of its 1960s predecessor. With a turbocharged 1.8-litre four-cylinder producing up to 300 bhp in S trim and weighing just 1,098 kg, it prioritises driving purity over outright power. The limited-run R and GT versions are the most sought-after.

The Aston Martin DB11, produced from 2016 to 2024, ushered in a new era with turbocharged engines replacing the naturally aspirated V12. Available with either a Mercedes-AMG-sourced 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 or Aston's own 5.2-litre twin-turbo V12, it combined cutting-edge aerodynamics with traditional craftsmanship.

The Aston Martin DB12, launched in 2023, is described by the company as the world's first super tourer. Its 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 produces 671 bhp, paired with an eight-speed automatic gearbox. A significant step up from the DB11, it features a new infotainment system and substantially revised chassis.

The Aston Martin DB5, produced from 1963 to 1965, is among the most famous cars ever made thanks to its starring role in the James Bond films. Powered by a 4.0-litre straight-six producing 282 bhp, just 1,059 were built. Immaculate examples now command prices well into seven figures.

The Aston Martin DB6, produced from 1965 to 1971, refined the DB5 formula with a longer wheelbase, improved aerodynamics, and a more spacious cabin. The Mk2 Vantage with its triple-Weber carburettors producing 325 bhp is the most desirable variant. Volante convertibles are exceptionally rare.

The Aston Martin DB7, produced from 1994 to 2004, was the model that saved Aston Martin. Initially powered by a supercharged 3.2-litre straight-six, the later Vantage variant received a 6.0-litre V12 producing 420 bhp. The V12 Vantage, particularly in manual guise, is the most collectible.

The Aston Martin DB9, produced from 2004 to 2016, was the first Aston Martin built on the VH aluminium platform. Its hand-assembled 6.0-litre V12 producing up to 540 bhp in GT spec, combined with Ian Callum's timeless design, defined a generation of British grand touring. Manual gearbox cars are rare and increasingly sought after.

The Aston Martin DBS, produced from 2007 to 2012, was the range-topping grand tourer based on the DB9 platform. Its naturally aspirated 6.0-litre V12 produced 510 bhp and was paired with a six-speed manual or Touchtronic automatic. The carbon-fibre-bodied DBS was notably the Bond car in Casino Royale and Quantum of Solace.

The Aston Martin DBS Superleggera, produced from 2018 to 2023, was the flagship grand tourer powered by a 5.2-litre twin-turbo V12 producing 715 bhp. The name revived the historic Superleggera badge, referencing the Touring of Milan lightweight construction technique. The 770 Ultimate edition is the most powerful.

The Aston Martin Valkyrie, delivered from 2021, is a hypercar developed in partnership with Red Bull Racing. Its Cosworth-built naturally aspirated 6.5-litre V12 combined with a hybrid system produces 1,155 bhp. Limited to 150 road cars, it was designed by Adrian Newey to bring Formula 1 aerodynamic technology to a road car.

The Aston Martin Vanquish has spanned two generations. The original (2001-2007) featured a 5.9-litre V12 and was the Bond car in Die Another Day. The second generation (2012-2018) used a 6.0-litre V12 producing up to 595 bhp in S form. Both are flagship GT cars combining handbuilt craftsmanship with V12 performance.

The Aston Martin Vantage spans three generations. The V8 Vantage (2005–2017) featured a naturally aspirated 4.3/4.7-litre V8 with a manual gearbox option. The 2018 relaunch brought a Mercedes-AMG 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 with 503 bhp. The 2025 facelift increased output to 656 bhp with revised styling.

generations.name: New Vantage
generations.image: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/51/2019_Aston_Martin_Vantage_coupe%2C_front_right_%28Wings-n-Wheels_2023%29.jpg/1920px-2019 Aston Martin Vantage coupe, front right (Wings-n-Wheels 2023).jpg

models.description: The Audi R8, produced from 2006 to 2024, is a mid-engined supercar that brought Le Mans-derived technology to the road. The first generation offered V8 and V10 engines; the second generation focused exclusively on the naturally aspirated 5.2-litre V10 producing up to 620 bhp. The manual-gearbox first-generation V10 is the most sought-after variant.

The Audi RS Q8, produced from 2019, is Audi Sport's flagship SUV sharing its platform with the Lamborghini Urus. Its 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 with mild-hybrid assistance produces up to 631 bhp in Performance trim, making it one of the fastest SUVs ever to lap the Nurburgring.

The Audi RS3, produced from 2011, is powered by Audi's iconic 2.5-litre five-cylinder turbo engine producing up to 400 bhp. Available as both Sportback and Saloon, it combines compact dimensions with extraordinary straight-line performance. The distinctive five-cylinder soundtrack is its calling card.

The Audi RS4, produced across multiple generations since 2000, is the high-performance version of the A4. The B5 generation featured a 2.7-litre twin-turbo V6, while the B7 introduced a naturally aspirated 4.2-litre V8 revving to 8,250 rpm. The B7 Avant with its howling V8 is the most collectible.

generations.name: B7
generations.credit: Ethan from Brooklyn, NY, U.S. / CC BY 2.0

generations.name: B9
generations.image: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Audi_RS4_Avant_B9_quattro_light_blue_-_front.jpg/1920px-Audi RS4 Avant B9 quattro light blue - front.jpg

models.description: The Audi RS5, produced from 2010, began with a magnificent naturally aspirated 4.2-litre V8 producing 444 bhp in the first generation. The second generation moved to a 2.9-litre twin-turbo V6 with 450 bhp. Available as Coupe and Sportback, the original V8 model is the purist's choice.

generations.name: B8
generations.image: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Audi_RS5_Coup%C3%A9_4.2_FSI_quattro_S_tronic.JPG/1920px-Audi RS5 Coupé 4.2 FSI quattro S tronic.JPG

models.description: The Audi RS6, produced across four generations since 2002, is the definitive super estate. The C6 generation's 5.0-litre twin-turbo V10 producing 572 bhp in standard form remains legendary. The current C8 with its 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 and up to 621 bhp in Performance trim continues the tradition.
models.heroEngine: 4.2L V8 TT / 5.0L V10 TT / 4.0L V8 TT

models.description: The Audi RS7, produced from 2013, is a four-door GT coupe combining supercar performance with grand tourer practicality. Its 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 produces up to 621 bhp in Performance trim, paired with quattro all-wheel drive and an eight-speed torque-converter automatic.

generations.name: C7
generations.credit: Charles from Port Chester, New York / CC BY 2.0

models.description: The BMW 1 Series M Coupe, produced in 2011 only, was a limited-run homologation special using a detuned version of the twin-turbo 3.0-litre six from the Z4 sDrive35is. With 335 bhp, wide arches, and a six-speed manual only, just 6,309 were built worldwide. It is widely regarded as the last true driver's BMW.

The BMW M1, produced from 1978 to 1981, was BMW's first and only mid-engined supercar. Designed by Giorgetto Giugiaro and powered by a 3.5-litre straight-six producing 277 bhp, just 453 road cars were built. Originally intended for Group 4 homologation, it remains one of the rarest and most desirable BMWs ever produced.

The BMW M2, produced from 2016, is the compact rear-wheel-drive M car. The original F87 used a 3.0-litre twin-turbo straight-six producing 365-405 bhp, while the G87 increased output to 460 bhp. The M2 CS and M2 Competition with their manual gearbox option are the most sought-after variants.

The BMW M3, produced in various generations since 1986, is BMW's iconic high-performance compact sports car. From the E30's motorsport-bred four-cylinder to the E46's legendary inline-six, the E90/E92's screaming V8, and the F80/G80's twin-turbo six, each generation has defined its era. The E46 CSL, E92 GTS, and manual-gearbox examples are the most collectible variants.

The BMW M4, produced from 2014, split from the M3 lineage as the two-door coupe and convertible variant. The F82 generation produced 425-444 bhp, while the G82 increased to 473-543 bhp in CSL form. The ultra-limited M4 CSL with its carbon roof and manual-delete weight saving is the ultimate version.

The BMW M5, produced in six generations since 1985, is the definitive super saloon. The E28 pioneered the concept, the E39 perfected it with its 4.9-litre V8, and the E60 introduced a screaming 5.0-litre V10. The E39 generation is widely regarded as the greatest sports saloon ever made.

The BMW M6, produced across three generations from 1983 to 2018, was BMW's flagship performance coupe. The original E24 with its motorsport-derived 3.5-litre straight-six was a Group A homologation special. The E63 introduced a 5.0-litre V10, and the F06/F12/F13 used a 4.4-litre twin-turbo V8 producing up to 600 bhp.

The BMW M8, produced from 2019, is powered by a twin-turbocharged 4.4-litre V8 producing up to 625 bhp in Competition specification. Available as a Coupe, Convertible, and four-door Gran Coupe, the M8 represents BMW's flagship performance grand tourer. The Competition variant with its enhanced power output and sharper chassis tuning is the most desirable.

The BMW Z4, produced across three generations since 2002, is BMW's two-seat roadster. The E85/E86 generation offered a folding hardtop coupe variant and the Z4 M with a 343 bhp straight-six. The current G29, co-developed with the Toyota Supra, offers up to 382 bhp in M40i form.

The BMW Z8, produced from 2000 to 2003, was a limited-production roadster designed by Henrik Fisker as a modern tribute to the 507. Its 4.9-litre V8 from the E39 M5 produced 394 bhp, paired exclusively with a six-speed manual. Just 5,703 were built, and values have risen dramatically.

The Ferrari 12Cilindri, produced from 2024, is Ferrari's front-engined V12 grand tourer succeeding the 812. With a naturally aspirated 6.5-litre V12 producing 819 bhp, it is available as a coupe and Spider.

The Ferrari 166 Inter, produced from 1948 to 1950, was among the very first Ferrari road cars. Powered by a Colombo-designed 2.0-litre V12, it established the template for decades of Ferrari grand tourers to come.
The Ferrari 195 Inter, produced from 1950 to 1951, succeeded the 166 Inter with a larger 2.3-litre Colombo V12. A rare early Ferrari road car, it bridged the gap between the 166 and the 212.

The Ferrari 208, produced from 1980 to 1989, was an Italian-market model with a 2.0-litre V8 to comply with tax regulations. Available as GTB and GTS, with later turbocharged variants offering significantly more power than the naturally aspirated versions.

The Ferrari 212 Inter, produced from 1951 to 1953, featured a 2.6-litre Colombo V12. Offered in both road and competition forms, the 212 helped establish Ferrari as a maker of desirable grand touring cars.

The Ferrari 250 California, produced from 1957 to 1963, is one of the most beautiful and valuable Ferraris ever made. A convertible variant of the 250 GT, both long-wheelbase and short-wheelbase versions are among the most sought-after collector cars in the world.

The Ferrari 250 Europa, produced from 1953 to 1954, was the first Ferrari to use the 3.0-litre Colombo V12 that would define the marque for decades. It laid the foundation for the legendary 250 GT series.

The Ferrari 250 GT, produced in many forms from 1954 to 1964, is the defining Ferrari grand tourer. Powered by the legendary Colombo 3.0-litre V12, variants include the Berlinetta, Cabriolet, California, passo corto (SWB), 2+2, and the exquisite Berlinetta Lusso.

The Ferrari 250 GTO, produced from 1962 to 1964, is widely considered the greatest Ferrari ever made. With only 36 examples built, the GTO is the most valuable car in the world, combining stunning Scaglietti bodywork with a race-bred 3.0-litre Colombo V12.

The Ferrari 275 GTB, produced from 1964 to 1968, was a landmark Ferrari with independent rear suspension and a rear-mounted transaxle. The later GTB/4 variant with its four-cam engine is among the most desirable Ferraris of the 1960s.

The Ferrari 275 GTS, produced from 1964 to 1966, was the open-top counterpart to the 275 GTB. With Pininfarina bodywork and a 3.3-litre Colombo V12, the GTS offered refined grand touring in convertible form.

The Ferrari 288 GTO, produced from 1984 to 1987, was Ferrari's first turbocharged road car and the spiritual predecessor to the F40. Built to homologate for Group B racing, only 272 were made. Its twin-turbocharged 2.9-litre V8 made it one of the fastest cars of its era.

The Ferrari 296, produced from 2022, marks the return of a mid-rear-engined V6 to Ferrari's road car range. Its 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 hybrid produces up to 830 bhp. Available as GTB, GTS, and the track-focused Speciale variants.

The Ferrari 308, produced from 1975 to 1985, is one of the most recognisable Ferraris ever made. Available as GTB (coupe) and GTS (targa), later variants included fuel-injected GTBi/GTSi models and the Quattrovalvole four-valve versions.

The Ferrari 328, produced from 1985 to 1989, succeeded the 308 with a larger 3.2-litre V8 and refined styling. Available as GTB (coupe) and GTS (targa), it is widely regarded as one of the most reliable and accessible classic Ferraris.

The Ferrari 330, produced from 1963 to 1968, was available as the GT 2+2, GTC coupe, and GTS spider. Powered by a 4.0-litre Colombo V12, the 330 family represented Ferrari's refined grand touring range of the mid-1960s.

The Ferrari 342 America, produced from 1951 to 1953, featured a larger Lampredi-designed 4.1-litre V12. Aimed at the American market, only six examples were built, making it one of the rarest early Ferraris.

The Ferrari 348, produced from 1989 to 1995, succeeded the 328 with a longitudinally-mounted 3.4-litre V8. Available as TB/TS (testa-mounted belt), later updated to GTB/GTS and Spider variants with improved handling and refinement.

The Ferrari 360 Modena, produced from 1999 to 2005, is powered by a naturally aspirated 3.6-litre V8 producing 394 bhp. The 360 introduced Ferrari's aluminium space-frame construction and represented a significant leap in design from its predecessor, the F355. Manual gearbox examples and the lightweight Challenge Stradale variant are the most sought-after by collectors.

The Ferrari 365 family, produced from 1966 to 1976, encompasses some of the most iconic Ferraris ever made. From the California spider to the legendary GTB/4 Daytona, the GT4 BB, and the comfortable GT4 2+2, the 365 range defined an era of Ferrari excellence.

The Ferrari 375 America, produced from 1953 to 1955, was powered by a potent 4.5-litre Lampredi V12 derived from the Formula One engine. With only 12 examples built, it remains among the rarest and most valuable Ferraris.

The Ferrari 400, produced from 1976 to 1985, was Ferrari's first car offered with an automatic transmission. A 2+2 grand tourer powered by a 4.8-litre V12, it was available as the Automatic and GT (manual), later updated to 400i with fuel injection.

The Ferrari 400 Superamerica, produced from 1960 to 1964, succeeded the 410 SA with a Colombo-derived 4.0-litre V12. Available in both coupe and cabriolet forms with various coachbuilder bodies, only 47 were made.

The Ferrari 410 Superamerica, produced from 1956 to 1959, was Ferrari's most powerful road car of its era. With a 5.0-litre Lampredi V12, it was the ultimate expression of Ferrari's grand touring philosophy in the 1950s.

The Ferrari 412, produced from 1985 to 1989, was the final evolution of the 400/400i series. A front-engined 2+2 grand tourer powered by a 5.0-litre V12, it was available with both manual and automatic transmissions.

The Ferrari 456, produced from 1992 to 2003, was a front-engined V12 grand tourer. Available as GT (manual) and GTA (automatic), with a mid-life update to the 456M. The 456 offered effortless performance and long-distance comfort.

The Ferrari 458 Italia, produced from 2009 to 2015, is powered by a naturally aspirated 4.5-litre V8 producing 562 bhp. Widely considered one of the greatest mid-engine Ferraris ever made, the 458 marked Ferrari's transition from the F1 automated manual to a dual-clutch gearbox. The track-focused Speciale variant is highly sought after by collectors.

The Ferrari 488 GTB, produced from 2015 to 2019, is powered by a twin-turbocharged 3.9-litre V8 producing 661 bhp. As Ferrari's first turbocharged mid-engine V8 since the F40, the 488 set new performance benchmarks. The Pista variant, with 710 bhp, is the most collectible version and commands a significant premium.

The Ferrari 500 Superfast, produced from 1964 to 1966, was the most expensive and exclusive Ferrari of its era. With a 5.0-litre Colombo V12 producing 400 bhp, only 37 were built. It was the ultimate luxury Ferrari grand tourer.

The Ferrari 512 BB, produced from 1976 to 1984, succeeded the 365 GT4 BB with a larger 5.0-litre flat-12 engine. Available as the carburetted BB and fuel-injected BBi, plus the rare BB LM competition variant. The mid-engined flagship defined Ferrari's top-of-the-range sports car.

The Ferrari 550 Maranello, produced from 1996 to 2001, marked Ferrari's return to a front-mounted V12 berlinetta. Its 5.5-litre V12 and perfectly balanced chassis made it an instant classic. The limited Barchetta Pininfarina open-top variant is exceptionally collectible.

The Ferrari 575M Maranello, produced from 2002 to 2006, succeeded the 550 with a larger 5.7-litre V12 and available F1-style automated gearbox. A refined grand tourer with improved power and technology, manual gearbox cars command a premium.

The Ferrari 599 GTB Fiorano, produced from 2006 to 2012, is powered by a front-mounted 6.0-litre V12 producing 612 bhp. The flagship grand tourer succeeded the 575M Maranello and remains one of the most celebrated front-engine V12 Ferraris. The later 599 GTO variant, limited to 599 units, commands a significant premium.

The Ferrari 612 Scaglietti, produced from 2004 to 2011, was Ferrari's front-engined V12 grand tourer. Named after legendary coachbuilder Sergio Scaglietti, its aluminium body and 5.7-litre V12 delivered effortless performance in a luxurious 2+2 package.

The Ferrari 812 Superfast, produced from 2017 to 2022, featured the most powerful naturally aspirated production V12 in history. Available as Superfast, GTS (retractable hard top), and the limited Competizione and Competizione Aperta track-focused variants.
The Ferrari 849 Testarossa pays homage to the legendary Testarossa lineage. Available as a coupe and spider, it continues Ferrari's tradition of iconic mid-engined grand tourers.
The Ferrari Amalfi is one of Ferrari's newest models, continuing the brand's tradition of combining cutting-edge technology with Italian design excellence.

The Ferrari California, produced from 2008 to 2017, was Ferrari's front-engined V8 grand tourer with a retractable hard top. Evolving through California, California 30, and the turbocharged California T, it combined open-top motoring with everyday usability.

The Ferrari Daytona SP3, produced from 2022, is part of Ferrari's Icona series paying homage to legendary racing prototypes. With a 6.5-litre V12 producing 829 bhp — the most powerful Ferrari internal combustion engine ever — and open-top targa bodywork, only 599 will be made.

The Ferrari Dino 206 GT, produced from 1968 to 1969, was the first mid-engined Ferrari road car. Powered by a 2.0-litre V6 with an aluminium body, only 152 were built before being replaced by the steel-bodied 246.

The Ferrari Dino 208 GT4, produced from 1975 to 1980, was a tax-friendly Italian-market variant of the 308 GT4 with a smaller 2.0-litre V8. Designed by Bertone, it shared the 308 GT4's Bertone-styled body.

The Ferrari Dino 246, produced from 1969 to 1974, succeeded the 206 GT with a larger 2.4-litre V6 and steel body. Available as the GT coupe and GTS targa, the 246 is one of the most beautiful Ferraris ever made and highly sought after by collectors.

The Ferrari Dino 308 GT4, produced from 1973 to 1980, was the first production Ferrari with a V8 engine and 2+2 seating. Designed by Bertone rather than Pininfarina, it featured a mid-mounted 3.0-litre V8.

The Ferrari Enzo, produced from 2002 to 2004, was named after the company's founder and represented the pinnacle of Ferrari road car technology. With a 6.0-litre V12 producing 651 bhp, Formula One-derived aerodynamics, and only 400 built, the Enzo remains one of the most celebrated supercars ever made.

The Ferrari F12 Berlinetta, produced from 2012 to 2017, is powered by a front-mounted 6.3-litre V12 producing 730 bhp. As the most powerful naturally aspirated Ferrari road car of its era, the F12 succeeded the 599 GTB and delivered supercar performance in a grand tourer package. The limited-run F12tdf variant is exceptionally collectible.

The Ferrari F355, produced from 1994 to 1999, is widely considered one of the finest mid-engine V8 Ferraris. Available as Berlinetta (coupe), GTS (targa), and Spider, the F355 combined a 3.5-litre V8 with sublime handling. Manual gearbox cars are particularly sought after.

The Ferrari F40, produced from 1987 to 1992, was the last Ferrari personally approved by Enzo Ferrari. Built to celebrate the company's 40th anniversary, its twin-turbocharged 2.9-litre V8, lightweight construction, and raw driving experience made it the definitive supercar of its era.

The Ferrari F430, produced from 2004 to 2009, features a naturally aspirated 4.3-litre V8 producing 483 bhp. Widely regarded as one of the finest mid-engine V8 Ferraris, well-maintained examples continue to hold strong value. Manual gearbox cars command a significant premium over F1 automated variants.

The Ferrari F50, produced from 1995 to 1997, was a thinly-disguised Formula One car for the road. With a 4.7-litre V12 derived from the 1990 F1 engine, carbon-fibre construction, and only 349 built, the F50 is one of the most exclusive supercars ever made.

The Ferrari F8, produced from 2019 to 2022, was the final evolution of Ferrari's mid-engine twin-turbo V8 sports car lineage that began with the 488. Available as Tributo (coupe) and Spider, it delivered 710 bhp from its award-winning 3.9-litre V8.

The Ferrari F80, announced in 2024, is the most powerful Ferrari ever made. Combining a 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 with three electric motors for a combined 1,200 bhp, only 799 will be built. It succeeds the LaFerrari as Ferrari's flagship hypercar.

The Ferrari FF, produced from 2011 to 2016, was Ferrari's first all-wheel-drive production car and its first four-seat grand tourer. With a 6.3-litre V12 producing 651 bhp and a unique shooting brake design, the FF was a revolutionary departure for Ferrari.

The Ferrari FXX, produced from 2005 to 2007, was a track-only development of the Enzo. With a 6.3-litre V12 producing 789 bhp and only 30 built, the FXX was part of Ferrari's Corse Clienti programme. It represents the extreme of Ferrari's engineering capabilities.

The Ferrari GTC4Lusso, produced from 2016 to 2020, succeeded the FF as Ferrari's four-seat grand tourer. Available with a 6.3-litre V12 (GTC4Lusso) or twin-turbo 3.9-litre V8 (GTC4Lusso T), it combined everyday usability with Ferrari performance.

The Ferrari LaFerrari, produced from 2013 to 2018, was Ferrari's first hybrid hypercar. Combining a 6.3-litre V12 with an electric motor for a combined 950 bhp, only 500 coupes and 210 Aperta open-top versions were made.

The Ferrari Luce is Ferrari's first all-electric vehicle, representing a bold new chapter for the marque. It combines Ferrari's design philosophy and performance DNA with zero-emission electric powertrain technology.

The Ferrari Mondial, produced from 1980 to 1993, was Ferrari's mid-engined 2+2. Evolving through Mondial 8, Quattrovalvole, 3.2, Cabriolet, and T versions, it offered four-seat practicality in a mid-engine package.

The Ferrari Monza SP1, produced from 2019, is a single-seat barchetta from Ferrari's Icona series. Powered by the most powerful Ferrari V12 at the time with 799 bhp, the SP1 pays homage to legendary Ferrari racing barchettas of the 1950s. Only 499 SP1 and SP2 combined were built.

The Ferrari Monza SP2, produced from 2019, is a two-seat barchetta from Ferrari's Icona series, sharing the SP1's 6.5-litre V12 producing 799 bhp. The SP2 adds a passenger seat and windscreen, offering a slightly more practical take on the open-top speedster concept.

The Ferrari Portofino, produced from 2018 to 2023, succeeded the California T as Ferrari's entry-level grand tourer with a retractable hard top. The updated Portofino M added an 8-speed gearbox and more power.

The Ferrari Purosangue, produced from 2023, is Ferrari's first four-door, four-seat car. Powered by a naturally aspirated 6.5-litre V12 producing 715 bhp, it features suicide rear doors, all-wheel drive, and active suspension.

The Ferrari Roma, produced from 2020, is a front-engined V8 grand tourer inspired by the carefree lifestyle of 1960s Rome. With elegant Pininfarina-influenced styling and a 3.9-litre twin-turbo V8, the Roma blends classic beauty with modern performance.

The Ferrari SF90 Stradale, produced from 2020, is Ferrari's plug-in hybrid supercar. Combining a 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 with three electric motors for a combined 986 bhp, it is the most powerful series-production Ferrari. Available as Stradale and Spider variants, plus the extreme XX versions.

The Ferrari 575 Superamerica, produced from 2005 to 2006, featured a unique electrochromic rotating roof that could transform from coupe to open-top in seconds. Based on the 575M, only 559 were built, making it a rare and innovative collector's piece.

The Ferrari Testarossa, produced from 1984 to 1996, is one of the most iconic supercars ever made. Evolving through Testarossa, 512 TR, and F512 M variants, its side-strake design and flat-12 engine defined the supercar genre of the 1980s and 1990s.

The Lamborghini Aventador, produced from 2011 to 2022, was the last Lamborghini flagship with a naturally aspirated V12. Its 6.5-litre engine produced 690-770 bhp across various iterations. The Ultimae, limited to 600 units, was the final version. As the last pure V12 Lamborghini, values are expected to appreciate.

The Lamborghini Countach, produced from 1974 to 1990, defined the supercar silhouette for a generation. Its scissor doors, wedge shape by Marcello Gandini, and longitudinally mounted V12 made it the ultimate bedroom poster car. The early LP400 Periscopio is the most sought-after among collectors.

The Lamborghini Diablo, produced from 1990 to 2001, succeeded the Countach as Lamborghini's flagship. Originally rear-wheel drive with 485 bhp, later VT models added all-wheel drive. The SE30, SV, and GT variants are the most collectible. It was the last Lamborghini developed before Audi ownership.

The Lamborghini Gallardo, produced from 2003 to 2013, was the most commercially successful Lamborghini ever with over 14,000 built. Its 5.0/5.2-litre V10 produced up to 562 bhp in Superleggera and Squadra Corse forms. The manual-gearbox LP560-4 and lightweight Superleggera are the most sought-after.

The Lamborghini Huracán, produced from 2014 to 2024, was the last Lamborghini powered exclusively by a naturally aspirated engine. Its 5.2-litre V10 produced up to 640 bhp in STO and Tecnica forms. The rear-wheel-drive STO, inspired by the Super Trofeo race car, is the ultimate road-going version.

The Lamborghini Miura, produced from 1966 to 1973, is widely considered the first true supercar. Its transverse mid-mounted 3.9-litre V12 by Giotto Bizzarrini and Marcello Gandini's sensational bodywork created an entirely new category of road car. The SV variant with 385 bhp is the ultimate expression.

The Lamborghini Murciélago, produced from 2001 to 2010, is powered by a naturally aspirated V12 displacing 6.2 to 6.5 litres and producing 572 to 661 bhp. As the last Lamborghini flagship to offer a gated manual gearbox, well-maintained examples — particularly the LP640 and LP670-4 SV variants — are increasingly sought after by collectors. Manual gearbox cars command a significant premium over E-Gear automated variants.

The Lamborghini Reventón, produced in 2007-2009, was a limited-edition supercar based on the Murciélago LP640 with fighter-jet-inspired bodywork designed by Manfred Fitzgerald. Its 6.5-litre V12 produced 641 bhp. Just 20 coupes and 15 roadsters were built, making it among the rarest modern Lamborghinis.

The Lamborghini Urus, produced from 2018, is the Italian marque's super SUV. Its 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 produces up to 666 bhp in Performante form, making it one of the fastest SUVs ever made. Sharing its MLB Evo platform with the Audi RS Q8, it has become Lamborghini's best-selling model.

The Lexus LFA, produced from 2010 to 2012, is powered by a hand-built 4.8-litre V10 producing 552 bhp, developed with Yamaha and revving to 9,000 rpm. Limited to just 500 units worldwide, the LFA is one of the rarest and most celebrated Japanese supercars ever built. The Nürburgring Package variant, limited to 50 units, is among the most valuable modern collectible cars.

The Lotus 2-Eleven, produced from 2007 to 2011, is an open-cockpit track car with a road-legal variant. Powered by a supercharged Toyota 1.8-litre engine producing up to 266 bhp in a car weighing just 670 kg, it was the fastest road-legal Lotus at the time. Around 400 were built across road and track specifications.

The Lotus 3-Eleven, produced from 2015 to 2017, was the fastest and most expensive road-legal Lotus of its era. An open-cockpit design powered by a supercharged 3.5-litre V6, it produced up to 450 bhp in track spec. Just 311 were built across road and race specifications.

The Lotus 340R, built in 2000, is a limited-edition open-top sports car based on the Elise platform. With its striking exposed body panels and Rover K-Series 1.8-litre engine producing 177 bhp in a car weighing just 701 kg, it offered supercar-rivalling performance. Exactly 340 were built, all pre-sold.

The Lotus Carlton, produced from 1990 to 1992, was the world's fastest production saloon at launch. Based on the Vauxhall Carlton, Lotus fitted a 3.6-litre twin-turbo straight-six producing 377 bhp and a top speed of 176 mph. Just 440 right-hand-drive examples were built, making it hugely collectible.

The Lotus Eclat, produced from 1975 to 1982, was the fastback sibling to the Elite. Sharing the same backbone chassis and 2.0/2.2-litre slant-four engine, its sleeker two-door body offered a sportier character. The Riviera and Excel-spec versions with improved interior trim are the most collectible.

The Lotus Elan, produced from 1962 to 1975, is regarded as one of the finest handling sports cars ever made. Its backbone chassis, all-independent suspension and twin-cam 1.6-litre engine set the template for every Lotus that followed. Available as both roadster and fixed-head coupe, with the Sprint being the most desirable variant.

The Lotus Elan M100, produced from 1989 to 1995, was a front-wheel-drive departure from Lotus tradition. Powered by an Isuzu 1.6-litre turbocharged engine, it was engineered to handle so well that its drivetrain layout was irrelevant. The SE turbo model is the one to have, with a loyal following among enthusiasts.

The Lotus Eletre, delivered from 2023, is Lotus's first SUV and first fully electric production car. Built on a dedicated EV platform, it produces up to 905 bhp in R+ guise with a 0-62 time of 2.95 seconds. Its active aerodynamics and lidar sensors represent a new chapter for the marque.

The Lotus Elise, produced from 1996 to 2021, revolutionised lightweight sports car design with its bonded aluminium chassis. Across three series it remained true to Colin Chapman's ethos of simplicity and lightness, with the Cup and Sport variants offering stripped-out track focus. One of the most popular Lotuses ever made.

The Lotus Elite (Type 75), produced from 1974 to 1982, was Lotus's ambitious move into the GT market. A 2+2 coupe with a fibreglass body bonded to a backbone chassis, it featured the Lotus 907 slant-four engine and offered a level of refinement new to the marque. It was the basis for the Eclat and later the Excel.

The Lotus Elite (Type 14), produced from 1957 to 1963, was the world's first fibreglass monocoque production car. Powered by a Coventry Climax 1.2-litre engine, it combined stunning aerodynamics with a featherweight chassis to dominate its class at Le Mans. Around 1,030 were built, making it a prized collector's piece.

The Lotus Emeya, delivered from 2024, is Lotus's all-electric four-door GT. Sharing its platform with the Eletre, it produces up to 905 bhp in R+ form and features active aerodynamics, lidar and a 102 kWh battery. It represents Lotus's vision for high-performance electric grand touring.

The Lotus Emira, launched in 2022, is Lotus's last petrol-powered sports car. Available with either a Mercedes-AMG 2.0-litre turbo four or Toyota 3.5-litre supercharged V6, it combines classic Lotus handling with a more refined interior. The V6 First Edition was the launch model, with the I4 following.

The Lotus Esprit, produced from 1976 to 2004, is one of the most recognisable supercars of the 20th century. Styled by Giorgetto Giugiaro and later Peter Stevens, it evolved from the wedge-shaped S1 through to the rounded V8 twin-turbo producing 500 bhp. Its appearance in The Spy Who Loved Me cemented its icon status.

The Lotus Europa, produced from 1966 to 1975, was the first affordable mid-engined road car. Its radical styling by Ron Hickman and race-bred chassis made it a favourite among enthusiasts. The twin-cam Special is the most sought-after variant, offering 126 bhp and a close-ratio five-speed gearbox.

The Lotus Europa S, produced from 2006 to 2010, was a GT car based on the Elise platform. Its 2.0-litre turbocharged engine and fixed-roof coupe body offered a more refined, long-distance alternative to the Elise and Exige. The SE model with 225 bhp and uprated brakes is the pick of the range.

The Lotus Evora, produced from 2009 to 2021, was Lotus's flagship mid-engined sports car. Powered by a Toyota 3.5-litre V6, the supercharged 400/410/GT430 variants delivered up to 430 bhp. Its blend of everyday usability with Lotus handling precision made it a genuine rival to Porsche's 911.

The Lotus Excel, produced from 1982 to 1992, was the final evolution of the Elite/Eclat platform. It featured the 2.2-litre slant-four engine mated to a Toyota five-speed gearbox, improving reliability considerably. The SE model with 180 bhp and the rare SA automatic are the most notable variants.

The Lotus Exige, produced from 2000 to 2021, is the hardtop, track-focused sibling of the Elise. Across three series it evolved from a Rover K-Series-powered lightweight to a supercharged Toyota V6 powerhouse. The final Sport 390 and Cup 430 editions are among the most focused road cars Lotus ever built.

The Lotus Seven, produced from 1957 to 1972, is the iconic lightweight sports car that defined the kit car genre. Designed by Colin Chapman, its minimalist aluminium-panelled body and tubular steel chassis delivered extraordinary performance at low cost. After Lotus ceased production, the design was licensed to Caterham Cars.

The Maserati Ghibli, produced from 2013 to 2023, was Maserati's mid-size executive saloon aimed at the BMW 5 Series and Mercedes E-Class. Powered by Ferrari-built 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 engines producing up to 580 bhp in Trofeo form, it combined Italian style with daily usability.

The Maserati GranTurismo, produced from 2007, is Maserati's flagship grand tourer. The first generation featured Ferrari-derived naturally aspirated V8 engines up to 4.7 litres, with the MC Stradale being the most focused variant. The second generation (2023) introduced Nettuno twin-turbo V6 and fully electric Folgore versions.

The Maserati MC12, produced from 2004 to 2005, was built to homologate the MC12 GT1 race car. Based on the Ferrari Enzo platform with a 6.0-litre V12 producing 622 bhp, just 50 were built — 25 road cars and 25 Corsa track versions. It is one of the rarest and most valuable modern supercars.

The Maserati MC20, produced from 2020, marks Maserati's return to mid-engined supercars. Its Nettuno 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 uses a pre-chamber combustion system derived from Formula 1 technology to produce 621 bhp. Available as Coupe and Cielo (convertible), it competes with the Ferrari F8 and McLaren 720S.

The Maserati Quattroporte, in its fifth and sixth generations (2004-2023), combined Italian flair with four-door practicality. The fifth generation's Ferrari-derived 4.2/4.7-litre V8 with a Duo-Select semi-automatic or six-speed manual is the most characterful. The Trofeo with its 580 bhp twin-turbo V8 was the ultimate version.

The McLaren 12C (originally MP4-12C), produced from 2011 to 2014, was McLaren Automotive's first production road car. Powered by a bespoke 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 producing up to 625 bhp, it featured a carbon fibre MonoCell chassis and was available as both coupe and spider.

The McLaren 540C, produced from 2015 to 2020, was McLaren's most affordable model and the entry point to the Sports Series range. With 533 bhp from the 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8, it offered genuine supercar performance with everyday usability.

The McLaren 570GT, produced from 2017 to 2021, was the grand touring variant of the Sports Series. Sharing the 570S's 562 bhp twin-turbo V8, it added a glass-roofed touring deck with 220 litres of luggage space, softer suspension, and enhanced sound insulation for long-distance comfort.

The McLaren 570S, produced from 2015 to 2021, was the most popular Sports Series model. With 562 bhp from the 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 and McLaren's signature carbon fibre MonoCell II chassis, it combined supercar dynamics with daily usability. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 600LT, produced from 2018 to 2021, was the second modern Longtail model. With 592 bhp, top-exit exhausts, and 96kg lighter than the 570S, it delivered an uncompromising track-focused driving experience. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 620R, produced from 2019 to 2021, was a road-legal version of the 570S GT4 race car. Limited to 350 units worldwide, it featured 611 bhp, full carbon fibre body panels, and race-derived aerodynamics including a large carbon fibre rear wing. A true road-legal race car.

The McLaren 625C, produced from 2015 to 2016, was a detuned version of the 650S designed primarily for Asian markets with softer suspension and a more comfort-oriented setup. With 625 bhp from the same 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8, it offered a more refined grand touring experience.

The McLaren 650S, produced from 2014 to 2017, replaced the 12C as the core of McLaren's Super Series. With 641 bhp from its 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 and styling influenced by the P1, it was available as both coupe and spider.

The McLaren 675LT, produced from 2015 to 2017, was the first modern McLaren to wear the Longtail badge. With 666 bhp and 100kg lighter than the 650S thanks to extensive use of carbon fibre, it delivered sharper, more track-focused performance. Available as coupe and spider, both limited to 500 units each.

The McLaren 720S, produced from 2017 to 2023, redefined the Super Series with a new 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 producing 710 bhp and the innovative Monocage II carbon fibre structure. Widely praised for its combination of performance, comfort, and dramatic design, it was available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 750S, produced from 2023, succeeds the 720S as the core of the Super Series. With 740 bhp from the upgraded 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 and 30kg lighter than its predecessor, it delivers even sharper performance and improved aerodynamics. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren 765LT, produced from 2020 to 2023, was the most extreme Longtail yet. With 755 bhp from the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 and 80kg lighter than the 720S, it delivered devastating track performance. Limited to 765 coupes and 765 spiders, both are highly sought after by collectors.

The McLaren Artura, produced from 2022, is McLaren's next-generation hybrid supercar. Featuring an all-new 3.0-litre twin-turbo V6 and electric motor producing up to 700 bhp, it rides on the new McLaren Carbon Lightweight Architecture. Available as coupe and spider.

The McLaren Elva, delivered from 2020, is a roofless, windscreen-less open-top roadster inspired by the original Bruce McLaren-Elva race cars of the 1960s. With 804 bhp and weighing just 1,148kg, it features an innovative Active Air Management System that deflects air over the cabin. Limited to 149 examples.

The McLaren F1, produced from 1992 to 1998, is one of the most iconic supercars ever built. Designed by Gordon Murray with a central driving position and a BMW-developed 6.1-litre V12 producing 627 bhp, it held the world speed record for a production car for over a decade. Variants include the ultra-rare LM and GT race homologation specials.

The McLaren GT, produced from 2019 to 2023, was McLaren's first true grand tourer. With 612 bhp from the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8, a glazed rear luggage bay offering 420 litres of storage, and a focus on long-distance refinement, it aimed to combine supercar performance with genuine GT usability.

The McLaren GTS, produced from 2024, succeeds the GT as McLaren's grand touring model. With 626 bhp from the 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8, it is lighter and more focused than its predecessor while retaining the practical luggage space and long-distance refinement that defined the GT.

models.heroCredit: Wikimedia Commons / Simon Davison / CC BY 2.0
models.description: The McLaren M6GT, built in 1969, was Bruce McLaren's attempt to create a road-going version of the dominant M6A Can-Am race car. Powered by a Chevrolet 5.0-litre V8, only three examples were completed before Bruce McLaren's tragic death in 1970, making it one of the rarest and most significant McLarens ever built.

The McLaren P1, produced from 2013 to 2015, is McLaren's first hybrid hypercar and a member of the "Holy Trinity" alongside the LaFerrari and Porsche 918. Combining a 3.8-litre twin-turbo V8 with an electric motor for 903 bhp, only 375 road cars were built. The track-only P1 GTR pushed output to 986 bhp.

The McLaren Senna, produced from 2018 to 2020, is the most extreme road-legal McLaren ever built. Named after Ayrton Senna, it produces 789 bhp from its 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 while weighing just 1,198kg dry. Limited to 500 road cars, with a further 75 track-only GTR variants producing even more downforce.

The McLaren Speedtail, delivered from 2020, is a three-seat hybrid hyper-GT and the spiritual successor to the F1. With 1,036 bhp from its hybrid V8 powertrain and a top speed of 250 mph, it features a central driving position, teardrop body shape, and flexible carbon fibre rear ailerons. Limited to just 106 examples.

The Mercedes-AMG C63, produced from 2008, is the high-performance C-Class. The W204 generation's naturally aspirated 6.2-litre V8 producing 451-507 bhp is the most characterful, while the W205's 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 offered 469-510 bhp. The 507 Edition and Black Series are the most sought-after.

The Mercedes-Benz CLK 63 AMG Black Series, produced from 2007 to 2009, was one of the first AMG Black Series cars. Its naturally aspirated 6.2-litre V8 produced 507 bhp, paired with wider bodywork, stiffer suspension, and rear-wheel drive. Just 700 were built worldwide, making it a rare and sought-after collector's item.

The Mercedes-AMG E63, produced from 2006, is the ultimate super saloon from Mercedes. The W212 generation with its naturally aspirated 6.2-litre V8 is the purist's choice, while the W213's 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 delivers 571-612 bhp with all-wheel drive. The E63 S Estate is the definitive Q-car.

The Mercedes-AMG GT, produced from 2014, is AMG's front-mid-engined sports car. Its hand-built 4.0-litre twin-turbo V8 produces 462-730 bhp depending on variant. The track-focused GT R with its rear-wheel steering and the limited-edition GT Black Series with 730 bhp are the most collectible.

The Mercedes-Benz SL 65 AMG Black Series, produced in 2008-2009, was the most extreme AMG Black Series ever. Its twin-turbocharged 6.0-litre V12 produced 661 bhp and 738 lb-ft of torque. Stripped of its folding roof and given a fixed carbon-fibre hardtop, just 350 were built. It remains one of the most sought-after modern Mercedes.

The Mercedes-Benz SLS AMG, produced from 2010 to 2015, was the spiritual successor to the 300SL with its iconic gullwing doors. Its hand-built 6.2-litre naturally aspirated V8 produced 563-622 bhp. The Black Series with 622 bhp and significantly reduced weight is the ultimate variant.

The Porsche 911, produced since 1963, is the definitive rear-engined sports car and one of the most iconic nameplates in automotive history. From the air-cooled 930 Turbo through to the modern 992, the 911 has evolved across ten generations while retaining its unmistakable silhouette and flat-six engine. Early air-cooled models — particularly the 930, 964 RS, and 993 — are among the most sought-after collector cars in the world.

The Porsche 918 Spyder, produced from 2013 to 2015, was Porsche's hybrid hypercar. Its naturally aspirated 4.6-litre V8 combined with two electric motors produced 875 bhp, enabling a Nurburgring lap time of 6:57. Just 918 units were built. The Weissach Package, saving 41 kg, is the most desirable specification.

The Porsche 928, produced from 1977 to 1995, was intended to replace the 911. Its front-mounted V8 and rear transaxle delivered GT-car refinement with sports car balance. The GTS with its 5.4-litre V8 producing 345 bhp is the most desirable. Originally unloved, prices have risen sharply as a modern classic.

The Porsche 944, produced from 1982 to 1991, was Porsche's best-selling model of the 1980s. Its front-mounted four-cylinder with rear transaxle delivered near-perfect 50/50 weight distribution. The Turbo S with 250 bhp is the most collectible, while the S2 with its 3.0-litre engine offers the best naturally aspirated performance.

The Porsche 968, produced from 1992 to 1995, was the final evolution of the transaxle Porsche line. Its 3.0-litre four-cylinder with VarioCam was the most powerful naturally aspirated four-pot of its day at 236 bhp. The Club Sport, stripped for track use, and Turbo S with 305 bhp are the most sought-after.

The Porsche Boxster, produced since 1996, is the mid-engined roadster that saved Porsche from bankruptcy. Across four generations (986, 987, 981, 718), it evolved from a 2.5-litre flat-six to include turbocharged four-cylinder and naturally aspirated 4.0-litre flat-six engines. The Boxster Spyder and 718 Spyder RS with the GT4 engine are the most collectible.

The Porsche Carrera GT, produced from 2004 to 2007, is one of the greatest analogue supercars ever made. Its mid-mounted 5.7-litre V10 derived from a stillborn Le Mans project produced 604 bhp, paired with a six-speed manual gearbox and no driver aids. Just 1,270 were built.

The Porsche Cayenne, produced since 2002, was the SUV that saved Porsche financially. Across three generations, it has offered everything from diesel efficiency to the 670 bhp Turbo GT. The first-generation Turbo S and current Turbo E-Hybrid are the performance highlights.

generations.name: E2
generations.image: https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/11/2015_Porsche_Cayenne_V6_Diesel_Triptonic_S_3.0_Front.jpg/1920px-2015 Porsche Cayenne V6 Diesel Triptonic S 3.0 Front.jpg

models.description: The Porsche Cayman, produced since 2005, is the fixed-roof sibling to the Boxster. Its perfect mid-engined balance and increasingly powerful engines have made it one of the best sports cars at any price. The 718 Cayman GT4 RS with its 4.0-litre flat-six from the 911 GT3 and 493 bhp is the pinnacle.

The Porsche Macan, produced since 2014, is Porsche's compact SUV and consistent best-seller. The GTS and Turbo with their 2.9-litre twin-turbo V6 offer serious performance, while the second generation introduces a fully electric powertrain with up to 639 bhp in Turbo trim.

The Porsche Taycan, produced since 2019, is Porsche's first fully electric car. Its dual-motor powertrain produces up to 952 bhp in Turbo GT form, with a unique 800V architecture enabling ultra-fast charging. Available as saloon, Sport Turismo, and Cross Turismo body styles.
